text: The video demonstrates the setup for a transperitoneal pelvic lymphadenectomy, including the creation of a pneumoperitoneum with a varus needle in the left hypochondrium and placement of an optical ten-millimeter trocar in the umbilicus. Three ancillary trocars are inserted: two medially to the anterior superior iliac spines and one in the suprapubic midline. The peritoneum is opened to access extraperitoneal spaces, with incisions extending from the parietal origin of the round ligament to the gonadal vessels crossing the external iliac vessels. The incision is made laterally to the broad ligament above the external iliac vessels, and the course of both ureters is identified to ensure their protection during dissection.

The surgeon dissects the psoas muscle along its epimysium, with the deep circumflex vein marking the caudal limit of dissection. The cephalic limit is the bifurcation of the common iliac artery. The dissection progresses along the lateral pelvic sidewall towards the puborectalis muscle, taking care to preserve the genitofemoral nerve, which is identified medially.

The surgeon dissects medially from the surface of the psoas muscle to the lateral side of the external iliac vessels. The lymph node block is pulled medially, creating a space between the external iliac vessels and the psoas and obturator muscles until the obturator nerve is reached. A window is created under the external iliac vessels during deep dissection, preparing the lateral meridian of the lymph node packet for removal.

The surgeon performs medial dissection to separate the medial side of the external iliac vessels from surrounding tissue and lymph nodes. The nodes between the external iliac vessels are mobilized for removal. The external iliac vessels are pulled laterally to facilitate dissection of the medial meridian of the lymph node packet. A window is created under the iliac vein to access the previously dissected area lateral to the iliac vessels and obturator nerve.

The surgeon carefully dissects the medial side of the external iliac vessels, ensuring the ureter, which distends onto the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, is not injured. The medial and lateral meridian of the lymph nodes packet is separated from surrounding structures. The paravesical fossa is dissected, with the umbilical artery serving as a key landmark. Dissection continues until the puborectalis muscle is reached, revealing the obturator nerve.

The surgeon performs dissection in the avascular space using pneumodissection, following the posterior site of the umbilical artery and internal iliac artery. Anatomical landmarks in the extraperitoneal space are identified, and the obturator, internal, and external iliac lymph nodes are laterally mobilized by dissecting the medial and lateral margins of the lymph node packet, without removing any lymph nodes.

The surgeon sections the distal caudal margin of the lymph node packet, exposing the pectineal ligament and coronamortis veins. The previously dissected obturator nerve is visible and preserved. Hemostasis is achieved by coagulating the lymphatic vessels. The surgeon performs obturator nerve stripping, gently detaching the lymph nodes from the nerve.

The surgeon sections the proximal margin of the lymph node packet, exposing the bifurcation of the common iliac artery. Bipolar cautery is used to dissect tissues and lymph nodes, ensuring hemostasis and closure of lymphatic vessels.

Pelvic lymphadenectomy is a standard oncology procedure. In this video, it is presented as a simple algorithm which respects the anatomy of the iliac fossa and the ergonomics of laparoscopic surgery. Standardization of practice is an important goal as it can reduce the rate of complications and harm to the patient. In our opinion, clearly described steps of the procedure, limited to a relatively small number, are easy to remember and help to ensure that the surgery is performed in the safe and certain way which apply to the most patients.
